recording_notes: DIFFERENT RECORDING SETUP — phone/camera filming an RCA monitor displaying the FLS trainer camera output. Image quality degraded by double-capture (trainer camera → monitor → phone). Different camera angle than V1-V10. Suture debris visible below trainer suggesting multiple prior attempts in this session. Confidence is reduced across all assessments due to image quality.

frame_analyses.description: At 10 seconds, instrument (silver) visible entering from right near Penrose drain on red foam suture block. Needle and suture visible. Quick entry.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Fast instrument entry consistent with trained performance. Monitor recording makes detail assessment harder.

frame_analyses.description: At 30 seconds, instrument visible near drain from right side. Drain and suture visible. No left instrument visible — may be below frame or at drain level.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture placement in progress. Camera angle through monitor makes instrument identification more difficult.

frame_analyses.description: At 50 seconds, both instruments visible near drain at close range, crossing over the drain surface. Needle driving appears in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Still in suture placement at 50 seconds. Extended placement phase consistent with ~50 second duration.

frame_analyses.description: At 70 seconds, right instrument elevated above drain with suture/needle visible at its tip. Left instrument below at drain level. Suture thread visible through drain tissue. Completing placement or repositioning needle.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Late suture placement or transitioning to first throw. Suture appears to have passed through drain. Approaching ~65 second placement time.

frame_analyses.description: At 90 seconds, clear throw wrapping visible. Right instrument elevated with suture looped around it. Suture X pattern visible on drain confirming placement through both marks. Classic surgeon's knot configuration.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: First throw wrapping in progress. Double-loop pattern visible despite monitor quality. Reaching first throw at ~80-85s.

frame_analyses.description: At 120 seconds, right instrument elevated with suture and needle visible at tip. Throw wrapping or tightening in progress. Suture knot forming on drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Second or early third throw. Instrument positions suggest continued throw phases. The curved needle is visible at the instrument tip.

frame_analyses.description: At 150 seconds, right instrument elevated with suture tails extending upward from drain. Knot visible on drain surface. Completing third throw or transitioning to cutting.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Late throw phase or early cutting. Suture tails extending upward suggest the knot is complete and preparation for cutting is beginning.

frame_analyses.description: At 170 seconds, suture tail visible extending from drain upward to right instrument. Cutting in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting phase. Still working on cutting suture tails at 170 seconds.

frame_analyses.description: At 190 seconds, suture tails still visible extending upward from drain. Right instrument (possibly scissors) visible holding suture. STILL CUTTING at 190 seconds — extremely extended cutting phase. Blue monitor artifact/scanline visible.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Extended cutting phase consuming 40+ seconds. Consistent with the cutting difficulty pattern seen in fatigued V8/V10 attempts.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 200 seconds. Instruments being withdrawn (one visible at right edge). Completed knot visible on Penrose drain — dark suture forming a knot pattern with short tails. The drain slit closure is DIFFICULT TO ASSESS from this monitor recording angle — the drain appears partially closed around the knot but gap assessment is unreliable at this image quality. Drain remains on suture block (not avulsed).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Task completed at approximately 195 seconds. The monitor-recorded image quality limits final result assessment. The knot appears present and compact. Drain not avulsed.

knot_assessments.notes: Frame 9 shows double-loop wrapping around right instrument consistent with surgeon's knot. Confidence low-moderate due to monitor recording quality.
Throw in progress at frames 12-15. Hand switch difficult to confirm through monitor recording. Confidence low.
Third throw completed. Knot appears present in final frames. Monitor quality limits detailed knot security assessment. Confidence low.

drain_assessment.assessment_note: Monitor recording quality makes gap assessment unreliable. The drain appears partially closed but this is the lowest-confidence drain assessment in the series.

technique_summary: This eleventh video uses a different recording setup — a phone/camera filming an RCA monitor displaying the FLS trainer camera output. This degrades image quality and limits assessment confidence. Despite this, the overall performance pattern is discernible: completion at approximately 195 seconds with an extremely extended cutting phase (~50 seconds). Needle loading remains fast at ~10 seconds. Suture placement at ~50 seconds is comparable to the pre-training baseline but slower than the V7/V9 peak of 30-33 seconds. The throw phases are individually reasonable (22-33 seconds each). The cutting phase at 50 seconds matches the worst attempts (V10: 50s) and remains the dominant time sink. If this is a different session from V1-V10, the 195-second time and ~50-second suture placement suggest the trainee performed this attempt without the benefit of warm-up, or it may represent continued fatigue effects.
improvement_suggestions: CUTTING remains the #1 issue at 50 seconds — dedicated scissors drills are critical. This has been the primary bottleneck in 4 of the last 5 videos.
Suture placement at 50 seconds has regressed from the V7/V9 peak of 30-33s — focus on consistent needle driving technique
Third throw at 33 seconds is extended — practice efficient wrapping for the final throw
Consider recording directly from the trainer camera rather than filming the monitor — this would significantly improve scoring accuracy for AI assessment
If this is a new session: limit to 3 attempts max and ensure warm-up before scored attempts
strengths: Needle loading at 10 seconds — maintained at peak efficiency across all sessions
First and second throw speeds (25s, 22s) remain at reasonable levels
All three throws completed in correct sequence
Drain not avulsed — consistent safe tissue handling

comparison_to_previous.recording_change: Different recording setup — phone filming RCA monitor. Lower confidence in all visual assessments.
key_observations: 195s completion — slightly faster than pre-training (198s) but far from V7/V9 peak (142-143s)
Cutting at 50s is now a persistent problem (V8: 38s, V10: 50s, V11: 50s)
Suture placement at 50s matches pre-training baseline — not V7/V9 level
Needle loading stable at 10s — fully retained
Different recording setup reduces scoring confidence significantly (0.42 vs typical 0.55-0.68)
Improved from V10 (215s → 195s) but still well below demonstrated capability
Recommendation: direct camera recording + session discipline (3 attempts max)
